constructs: semantic association, spreading activation, implicit memory, false report
claim: Participants develop vivid, confident false memories of events (unpresented words) that never occurred, driven by automatic associative processes rather than conscious reconstruction.

phenomenon: Coupling between temporal and sequential information in working memory
constructs: timing signal, order representation, context renewal, grouping
claim: Time and order are jointly represented at multiple levels; temporal information influences order recall and vice versa, but the weighting between them is task-dependent rather than fixed.

constructs: partial information, guessing bias, task design sensitivity, lexicality
claim: Whether repetition blindness appears for nonwords depends critically on whether task structure allows participants to use partial information to guess; this confound, not cognitive differences, explains prior conflicting findings.

phenomenon: Learning as impediment to behavioral prediction
constructs: mental model adaptation, Bayesian updating, task diagnosticity, individual differences
claim: Learning processes that help participants adjust to task structure simultaneously increase noise in individual differences, making ill-defined tasks poorer at predicting real-world behavior than well-defined tasks despite normative reasoning improvements.

constructs: occurrence judgment, causal strength, cause prevalence, ambiguity resolution
claim: Causal judgments prioritize explaining observed outcomes rather than computing objective contingencies; when causes compete in explaining occurrences, prevalence shifts attribution even when contingency favors a different cause.

phenomenon: Quantitative capacity differences manifest as qualitative effect reversals
constructs: feature interference, attention scaling, individual capability, effect distribution
claim: Phonological similarity and word-length effects vary from robust to reversed based on overall memory ability without requiring different encoding strategies; quantitative cognitive parameters produce apparent qualitative shifts.
